sentences.text: Well, we knew, as I said, from-- we heard artillery fire.
We heard from the things I picked up on the radio that it was done, that I could hear.
And the people-- there was connection with the outside.
The supplies had to be bringed in-- brought in, and people went out.
And Schindler probably knew what was going on.
And it was a very exciting time.
It was a very difficult time, because there was a question-- it was the uncertainty, would anything happen to us in the last minute?
Because all the other camps, they were liquidated, [INAUDIBLE] marchers.
And we were lucky that we were in this part of-- part of Czechoslovakia, where they couldn't really send us anywhere anymore.
And-- but then there was a question, if we survive, what are we going to do?
All of a sudden, you have been a prisoner for so many years, so all of a sudden, you're let loose on civilization.
What kind of civilization do we have here?
And what are we going to do?
Where are we going to do?
How are we going to do?
You know, very, very difficult times.
In this particular period of time, in April of '45, as you were going through all these concerns, you decide to start to keep a diary.
Yes.
I don't know why.
Maybe because I had a pencil and I had some-- some notebooks that I had.
And I wrote it in  German shorthand.
I figured, if it was discovered, it wouldn't be so easy for them to read.
I think it was history.
And it's-- I started it.
What I was going to do with it, I don't know.
In Prussia, I wouldn't have dared doing this.
But there, you felt pretty safe under Schindler.
And-- and I did it.
And I started around 21st or 22nd April that I started-- started writing.
Until we came to Italy, I think.
But then, I had to stop because too many things were happening.
I kept it for-- for a few months.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I tried to translate some of it.
Here's an entry that I have on April 25.
That was about 10 days, or 20-- 15 days before the liberation.
Here, I translate.
This is what I wrote.
'Who will ever forget these exciting days?
Days of uncertainty and of doubts.
Depending on what news comes through to us, a decision has to come soon, but be-- it can be a question of minutes, or even days, or weeks.
Nobody knows what it will be like.
It could mean death.
It could mean trouble again, which would be the same.
Maybe the war action will hit us here in Brinnlitz.
Maybe things will happen that nobody can predict.
We know one thing.
The war in general and our suffering are on the last stages.
It's like a crisis we are going through.
It will be either death, or the return to a free life, if there will ever be a free life again.
But there will be no in-between.
For lunch, we received an extra half a loaf of bread.
The women, a loaf-- the women have a loaf-- no, we received a extra quarter loaf of bread.
The women half a loaf.
Oh, boy, what more do you want?
There are rumors that Brinnlitz is in the center of a pocket amid the military action around us.
And then, the announcement from the director.
If the [YIDDISH] will still last until Shabbos, you will all get an extra half of bread, canned meat, and tobacco.
Saturday will be his birthday.
Now if the [YIDDISH]-- this is the wedding, if the wedding-- he meant the whole thing will still last until Shabbos, until Saturday.
You will all get an extra half a bread, canned meat, and tobacco.
Saturday was his-- will be his birthday.
We are all going crazy.
Nobody wants to stand in line for the midday soup.
We are all impatient and tense.
Is this what the end will look like?
And for the evening meal, cucumber, margarine, and bread.'
That was my entry on April 21.
I have in my diary, which I don't have here, of course.
I had an entry when-- later, when Schindler spoke to us, and-- and how we woke him up on his birthday.
And he came out and we played songs of the Viennese [INAUDIBLE].
And his wife stuck her nose our first, out of his room.
And she saw all the-- the whole camp assembled in front there.
And-- and she was through.
And he came out after 10 minutes in a white silk shirt, very clean and very civilized looking.
He smelled from a good perfume, well-groomed.
And we all wished him happy birthday.
And they-- they-- they gave him some birthday presents that were homemade.
And then, he spoke to us and how-- expressed it.
Well, that's what I-- he had well wishes for us.

And he wouldn't have thought it was possible that, in this stage of the war, after six years, we could still be there and that-- not be somewhere in the woods and-- and chase some impossible ideals, and that you will soon go free.
And he asked us not to pass judgement, to accuse.
We have to excuse, but let other people do-- do the judging.
And-- and-- and he wished us all the best.
And he told us that the-- the magazines are-- are full of clothes, and that the [NON-ENGLISH] will have instructions how to distribute it.
And we should not do any looting, any robbing, or any killing.
And he did-- at this point, he did not know yet what he was going to do.
Well, at the surrender of Germany, he had left already.
Because--
He left on the 7th, I think.
The Germans left on the 8th.
And I think that Germany did not surrender, until the 8th.
But-- yeah?
I think he left on the 8th.
On the night of the 8th.
No, not really.
He took-- it happened at night.
And it was-- and we knew he took some people along with him.
They put him and his wife in-- in prison camp uniforms and put him in the back of the car.
They took along a-- they loaded up the truck.
There was a truck, or two trucks.
And then, I think, he had seven or eight Jews that went with him, partly to protect him, and partly he needed-- he needed the help.
May 10th.
The first Russian came in May 10th.
Well, he came in on a horse, riding in by himself.
And, of course, he spoke Russian.
And most of us didn't speak Russian, but he got the idea.
And-- and he said-- well, he has to go back to his headquarters and has to report it.
And we should stay quiet, not leave the camp, and trust the men to avoid any accidents.
I mean, there was an accident on one day, almost.
There was some withdrawn German troops that was over our courtyard.
There was a railroad siding.
And over it, was a highway.
The withdrawing German troops are going-- hiding there.
And they saw us, and they started shooting into the camp.
So it was-- it was not quite all that safe yet.
Nothing.
Let them go away.
We-- we wanted to be safe.
Yes.
We had weapons-- the-- I understand, the-- the partisans in the-- who were in the woods, had weapons smuggled in.
So there was certain groups of-- of prisoners, who came from certain areas from the country, were more aggressive than the people who came from Krakow, who were city people.
They were more used to using weapons.
They were-- they were tougher.
And there was one group from [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were-- they sort of took over.
And soon as the Germans left, they formed like a camp militia.
They-- they called it the entrance.
And they-- they-- they-- we had weapons, which we would have used, if necessary.
Not me, I wouldn't know how to use them.
[CHUCKLES]
Well, they came back the next day, and they-- they gave us a-- a party.
It's an unimportant thing.
And they-- in fact, they set up a long white table with two or three bottles of vodka.
And they started to talk in-- in-- in-- in-- in-- in Russian, and cheering Stalin, and the victorious Army, and so on.
And we didn't know what the hell was going on.
[LAUGHS] But they gave us food.
They told us, yeah, go into a house.
Help yourself to a [INAUDIBLE], whatever.
They gave us food, but we were not comfortable.
We just were not comfortable.
Yeah.
Well, we-- we gradually started venturing out.
We went for a little walk outside the-- the gates of the camp, and then into the houses.
Then we looked around, and we were not  cut out to-- to do any looting or burn any homes, deserted homes.
And we tried to think what to do.
And the-- the Russians investigated-- no, interrogated us.
And where are you from?
If you are from-- from Krakow, from Dresden, you go back to Dresden.
That's-- that's the way they did it.
We did not want to go back to Dresden.
Since we were in Czechoslovakia, we decided to make it to Prague, where my sister-- my sister's fiance had lived before the war.
We were trying to see if he was still alive.
And we finally made our way to Prague.
And that was not-- it was easy, either, because there really was no-- no transportation available.
You had to jump on a train somewhere, and hope not to get-- get thrown off.
We finally made it to Prague.
And we stayed in Prague for two weeks.
My sister's fiance and his-- his family were all wiped out, did not come back.
But the Prague-- the Czech people were very, very nice to us.
There is one incident-- we were standing on the railroad-- did I tell you this?
And-- but not in the taping.
We-- we stood on the railroad, my father, my mother, my sister, and I.
And a man, a Czech man-- of course, we didn't speak any Czech-- comes up to my father, grabs him on his arm, and takes him aways.
And after 20 minutes, my father came back in a civilian suit.
We still had our uniforms on.
That man took him in his office, nearby.
He gave him a-- a suit, one of his own suits.
He had him change right in his office there.
He had a-- he carried his old uniform in a bundle.
And-- and he-- he sent him back.
We didn't know his name.
Later on, we found the building was in a-- in the chamber of commerce building.
That's how the Czech people were.
They would stop us on the street-- we were walking around in uniform-- we looked in a pastry shop, and they come, which one do you like?
They went in and bought it for us.
Or they brought us some-- some food.
And we spent quarters, what we had in quarters, first in a-- first, I think, first [INAUDIBLE] we spent in a movie house that was open at night.
Prague was a very busy place, because there were people from all over Europe trying to get home-- a lot of Italians, a lot of Yugoslavs.
It was-- it was a big-- big mess.
Everybody was around and looking for-- looking for family, and so on.
And then, we were in a-- a-- I don't know where we were, somewhere.
We stayed in several places.
I think we got some money for food from the-- from the Prague-- we had to register there.
And but people-- young people walked around all over.
I mean, they saw us, where were you?
Have you seen my brother?
My uncle, or my father?
They searched for people.

And when they saw my parents, and they saw-- saw my parent-- my mother, white-haired, and in her 50s, my-- my father, you know, white-hair and elderly-- for that time was an elderly gentleman-- they said, how come your parents are alive, and my parents are not alive?
And that was a-- you almost get a guilt feeling that you are alive.
It's understandable, but it was unpleasant.
And the same happened to us later in-- in Modena and Italy.
Again, people traveled all over looking for relatives.
It-- it was-- it was a terrible thing.
In Prague, well, we got some food in Prague.
We were very much down.
Fear.
I would say it was fear.
85. 85 pounds.
You saw, on some of these pictures, what I looked like.
And my mother, I think, had it tougher.
She worked hardest, because she had to work-- she had worked the night shift.
And she-- she worked harder than all of us, I think, physically.
And I remember, in Prussia, sometimes, she gave m a piece of her bread.
She gave it to me, figured I was-- was more hungry.
You know, you had a-- that's what happened.
Yes.
[SIGHS] We had-- we heard, in Prague, things are good at Linz, Austria.
Over there, you know, it's always-- the glass is always greener on the other side of the fence.
So we really had nowhere to go.
We would not go back to Germany, so we went to try to get to Linz.
And there was supposed to be a train from Prague to Linz, one a day.
And-- and we-- what?
We came in to the-- to the station.
And when we came to the station, we didn't know that train didn't run on Sunday, so we took another train that brought us near the Czech border.
And there we-- again, we-- we could not go any further.
That train, all civilians had to leave the train, because the train-- two-- two cars went on to the border, across the-- the zones.
And it was only for official personnel.
And somewhere, my father talked the people into-- to leave us on these two cars.
And there were four young fellows, who attached themselves to us.
They always liked my father, because they all had lost their parents.
My father was like an elder-- elder man that they could consult.
And they always came to him and asked him for advice.
And they-- we were glad to have them with us.
And we landed in a little town called, [NON-ENGLISH] near the Czech-- near the Austrian-- near the Austrian border.
And the station master, like one-- one-- one room station house, took us in.
Let us stay in the station house overnight.
And we were there for two weeks.
And there, the Czechs were very good.
And there, we got food.
Brought-- people bought food [INAUDIBLE].
We saw the first American there.
It was in the American zone.
One day, somebody knocked at the door.
A-- a Jew-- an American officer.
Are there Jews here?
It was the first Jewish-American soldiers that we met.
And he brought us-- next day, he brought us food.
And nearby, was an Italian and Yugoslav transit camp with some Italian and Yugoslav soldiers.
Lived in tents, until they can be repatriated.
And we walked over there.
We could not communicate, but one of the soldiers took a liking to my sister and said, well, if you ever come to Rome, come to mia casa-- come to my house.
And gave us his address.
And sure enough, a few weeks later, we came to Rome.
And my father and I went in there.
We-- we went in his store.
It was a little apartment in the basement of a little [CHUCKLES] apartment house.
And a woman opened the door.
And we asked for Senor [?
Beshalia. ?] Said no, [?
Beshalia ?] is in the war.
I don't know whether he is alive.
And I told her, well, we have seen your husband two weeks ago in-- in-- in Czechoslovakia.
And we had to move in with her.
We had no place to go.
Then, we went Back to [NON-ENGLISH], and we picked up our women.
We moved in there, like the size of our kitchen, a little area, where she made up.
The four of us slept there, and she cooked for us.
And then, her husband came back after a few-- we-- we stayed with these people for two months.
They were very nice.
Italian people, difficulty communicating, and-- but that's what we-- that's what we did.
And then--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, from-- from Linz-- we finally made our way into Linz, and there was a DP camp.
And outside of Linz--
The name [SIGHS] was Guthart, G-U-T-H-A-R-T, in Leonding, L-E-O-N-D-I-N-G, outside of Linz.
It was a DP camp.
There, we met lot of our people from Prussia again.
We somehow-- we somehow got there.
And we were very unhappy in Linz.
It was a Austrian population there who-- we were-- didn't have nothing to eat, no place to sleep.
And they were sitting in their homes and looking down on us, sort of laughing, or so, on us.
And it was a bad atmosphere.
The-- the-- the-- the food was bad, and sleeping quarters were very bad.
And so we were restless.
So that's-- we decided, let's go to-- to Salzburg.

And until the DP camp, Soldiers of the Palestine Brigade is where Jewish soldiers in the British uniform-- they worked for the British-- the worked for the British Army-- came there to-- to promote people going to Palestine.
And they arranged transports out of Italy into Palestine, some of them illegal.
And they left out of Salzburg.
And we tried to make it to Salzburg.
Another-- another little incident there that-- we left Linz.
We left Linz.
And the next day, we stood on the street.
Traveling, you stood on the street at the end-- outside-- the outskirts there, and trucks would take you along.
You'd jump up in the truck.
And after one day, we came to a small town.
And they arrested us.
We were waiting for a truck.
They arrested us.
We did not know that-- that a-- a-- a day before, an order was issued that only Austrian citizens were allowed to travel without any permits, and others not.
So they took the four of us, or the eight of us, they put us up in a school.
And that little town, was Wells, W-E-L-L-S.
They kept us there for three days.
They gave us food, and then they put us on the truck and sent us back to Linz.
[CHUCKLES] And after a couple of days, we went by truck from Salzburg.
We went with a truck from Leonding to Salzburg in a transit camp, again, for Austrian, and for Italian, and prisoners.
And we were there for-- for four days-- no, a few days.
And then, we went on by train, to-- supposedly to Italy.
Next morning, we were-- we-- we got a train.
[INAUDIBLE] we were in Munich.
These were all cattle cars, again, [INAUDIBLE].
And it was raining.
And the-- it rained into the-- into the cattle cars, and which we didn't mind it, really, because we were free.
So what?
What's a little rain to us?
And-- and from Munich, we went to Mittenwalde, a town in-- in upper Germany.
We were, again, in three days in a transit camp.
And then, we went by train, via Innsbruck, the Brenner Pass, to Bologna, where we spent two days.
And there was another incident on this, near-- near Verona.
The train stopped overnight on the track.
And we didn't have much food.
We [INAUDIBLE].
Some young fellows started scouting out.
And-- and they came across a farm.
And the farm had a barn full of peaches that were too ripe-- too ripe to be sold.
And the farmer gave us permission to take the peaches.
Meanwhile, the whole train, it-- with bags, whatever bags we had, went to that farm.
We filled everything up with peaches, that we can.
And it was the most delicious peaches we ever had in our life.
Many of us had a bad belly ache, too, after that.
We were not used to this food.
But it was one of the incidents.
The whole trip into Italy was a great experience, because we were free.
The-- the cattle cars did not have any barbed wire on it.
The-- the-- the doors were open.
Every couple of hours, in a free and open country, the train would stop, blow the whistle.
Everybody would jump off the train, disappear in the bushes.
And after five minutes, they would blow the whistle again.
Everybody got back into the train.
And it's not always easy to get into a cattle car.
I almost missed the train once on one of these occasions.
But that was nice.
And then we finally came to Bologna.
And then, we were in a DP camp in Modena, which was about 30 miles north of Bologna.
It was a former military academy.
We stayed there from July-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from July, 18, I think, to September 27th.
There were about 4,000 Jews there and-- and some other nationals.
And this was a bad camp, again.
There was-- these things-- you know, when we were liberated, we thought, well, the world would have understanding for us and would try to-- to help us.
Well, they did, but there-- it was a great disappointment, the way we were being pushed around.
In the camp, we were guarded.
If we wanted to go out, we-- if we wanted to sell a shirt or something, go out sell it on the black market, we could not.
We had to-- they searched our-- they searched us, when we went out.
Sleeping was very, very bad.
It-- it wasn't very good.
There was a contingent of Greek Jews there.
And it was-- it-- it-- it was unpleasant, and so we had to go on.
So we started-- my father and I started to-- to scout on our-- we went to Milan first, but conditions weren't much better there.
And we went back to Modena.
Then we went to Florence, and it was a little better.
But we finally decided to go to Rome, because we felt the potential in Rome was much better to-- to-- to get.
And in Modena, I counted about 60 to 80 people on a stone floor, sleeping there.
And again, people looking for their relatives.
It-- it-- it was-- it was very, very unpleasant.
Yeah.
That your sister had-- had been interested in your sister and stayed with them for two months.
We all-- no, we all stayed with that family.
For two months, yeah.
And the woman, she wouldn't let us go.
The woman had to cook.
And-- and of course, we tried-- from the little money we got, we tried to contribute to-- to-- to whatever we could, until we went on our own then.
And I got a job with the-- with the British Army unit, a civil labor unit, the British Civilian Labor Office.
Office.
I started as a-- as an office clerk.
And before I came to America I was a senior administrative assistant.
That was the office work.
And by that time, with my English, it-- it-- it got-- got better.
And-- and besides this, I had a job with the Universal International, the American film company, or Universal Pictures, it was called again.
They had an office in Rome, the Italian distributorship for-- for their movies.
They needed somebody who could talk fluently, who knew English shorthand fluently.
Well, I didn't know English shorthand fluently, but-- I didn't know English fluently, but I know German shorthand fluently.
And I was able to adjust it.
And-- and I became-- was the secretary, a temporary secretary for the Italian manager of Universal.
And it was really interesting.
They exported Universal Pictures there, and we dubbed them there in their own dubbing office.
And the-- the-- the distributor through the country came around.
And I worked these two-- two jobs, until we-- we finally could come to America.
We came to America in February, '45.
No.
[INAUDIBLE] I'm sorry. '47.
February-- February, 20, we arrived in 1947.
